title: Placental Abruption
chief_complaint: I'm 34 weeks pregnant. I have severe constant abdominal pain and dark red vaginal bleeding. It started suddenly after I slipped on ice. The baby isn't moving as much.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Tender, firm, woody-hard uterus (Couvelaire uterus - pathognomonic). Fundal height 38cm (increased - concealed hemorrhage). Hypertonic contractions q2min.
physical_exam.Pelvic: Dark red vaginal bleeding (non-clotted). Cervix closed, long. No placenta previa on prior US.
physical_exam.Fetal: Fetal bradycardia 80 bpm (ominous - fetal distress). Minimal variability. Late decelerations.
physical_exam.Extremities: Cool, pale. Petechiae on arms (DIC developing).

hidden_data.cbc: Hb 7.5 (acute blood loss), Platelets 70,000 (consumption), Fibrinogen 120 (low - DIC), INR 2.5, PT 28, PTT 55
hidden_data.ob_us: Large retroplacental hematoma 8cm (concealed abruption). Placenta posterior. No placenta previa. Fetal HR 80 bpm. Absent fetal movement. BPP 2/8 (ominous).

correct_diagnosis: Severe Placental Abruption (Grade 3 - Concealed Hemorrhage) with DIC and Fetal Distress - OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: Placental abruption = premature separation of placenta from uterine wall before delivery. Leading cause of perinatal mortality (15-20%). Presents with vaginal bleeding (80% - dark red), uterine tenderness/hypertonus, fetal distress

Risk factors: Trauma (most common acute cause), Hypertensive disorders (preeclampsia, chronic HTN - most common predisposing), Cocaine use, Smoking, PPROM, Prior abruption (10% recurrence), Thrombophilias, Advanced maternal age

Classification: Grade 1 (mild): Minimal bleeding, no fetal distress, Dx by clot on placenta after delivery. Grade 2 (moderate): External bleeding, uterine tenderness, fetal distress. Grade 3 (severe): Concealed hemorrhage, uterine tetany, DIC, fetal death

DIC from abruption: Placental thromboplastin enters maternal circulation → massive consumption of clotting factors and platelets → DIC. Treat underlying cause (DELIVER). Replace clotting factors (FFP, Cryoprecipitate, Platelets) DURING delivery, not before

Management: EMERGENCY CESAREAN SECTION for fetal distress (no vaginal delivery if fetus alive and in distress). Vaginal delivery if: Fetus dead, Cephalic presentation, Adequate pelvis, No maternal contraindication. Amniotomy: Releases pressure, may slow DIC progression. Aggressive fluid resuscitation, blood products, MTP if needed. Oxytocin after delivery (prevent postpartum hemorrhage). Monitor for postpartum hemorrhage (atonic uterus fails to contract - DIC + Couvelaire uterus)

Complications: Maternal: DIC, Hemorrhagic shock, Postpartum hemorrhage, Renal failure, Sheehan syndrome (pituitary necrosis from hypotension), Death (rare in developed countries). Fetal: Hypoxia, Perinatal death (15-20%), Cerebral palsy, Preterm birth complications
